name: Jerez de los Caballeros and Valle de Matamoros — circular from Oliva de la Frontera
name_original: Jerez de los Caballeros y Valle de Matamoros — circular desde Oliva de la Frontera
region_intro: Road cycling around Oliva De La Frontera, a municipality in Extremadura, offers diverse landscapes for cyclists. The region is characterized by vast expanses of dehesa pastureland, featuring gentle, rolling shapes and a mix of open fields and oak forests. Cyclists will also encounter sierras, or mountain ranges, cut by small valleys, providing varied terrain and more challenging climbs. The Ribera del Río Ardila forms a significant natural feature, offering riverine landscapes. Best road cycling routes around Oliva De La Frontera The most popular road cycling route is Jerez de los Caballeros and Valle de Matamoros — circular from Oliva de la Frontera, a 48.7 miles (78.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 11 minutes to complete. This difficult route features challenging climbs and varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Villanueva del Fresno loop from Oliva de la Frontera, a moderate 39.8 miles (64.1 km) path. This route traverses rolling dehesa pastureland and oak groves. Local road cyclists also love the Zahínos Tower loop from Oliva de la Frontera, a 37.9 miles (61.1 km) trail leading through quiet secondary roads and offering scenic views, often completed in about 2 hours 54 minutes. Road cycling around Oliva De La Frontera is defined by dehesa pastureland, oak forests, and sierras cut by small valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more demanding ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Oliva De La Frontera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Oliva de la Frontera?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape for road cyclists. You'll find vast expanses of 'dehesa' pastureland with gentle, rolling shapes and a mix of open fields and oak forests, ideal for smoother rides. For those seeking more challenge, the area also features 'sierras' (mountain ranges) cut by small valleys, providing varied terrain with demanding climbs and magnificent views.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Oliva de la Frontera?
A: There are 9 distinct road cycling routes around Oliva de la Frontera listed in this guide, catering to various preferences and skill levels. These routes explore the diverse landscapes of the region, from rolling dehesa to more challenging mountain ascents.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, while some routes feature significant climbs, there are moderate options available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31843968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva del Fresno loop from Oliva de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 64.1 km path that traverses rolling dehesa pastureland and oak groves. Another moderate choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31466014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zahínos Tower loop from Oliva de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through quiet secondary roads.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991792212/jerez-de-los-caballeros-and-valle-de-matamoros-circular-from-oliva-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jerez de los Caballeros and Valle de Matamoros — circular from Oliva de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; offer difficult terrain with significant climbs and varied landscapes over 78.4 km. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Higuera la Real – Convent of the Poor Clares loop from Oliva de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt;, which spans over 121 km with substantial elevation gain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views of the 'dehesa' pastureland, oak forests, and the 'sierras'. The Ribera del Río Ardila also provides impressively beautiful riverine landscapes. You might also pass by points of interest such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2799550" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zaos Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5326971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zahínos Tower&lt;/a&gt;, which is part of a popular loop route.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Oliva de la Frontera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991792212/jerez-de-los-caballeros-and-valle-de-matamoros-circular-from-oliva-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jerez de los Caballeros and Valle de Matamoros — circular from Oliva de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31843968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva del Fresno loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31466014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zahínos Tower loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Oliva de la Frontera?
A: The region's climate makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and lush landscapes. Summers can be very hot, especially in July and August, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended if cycling during these months. Winters are generally mild, though some days can be cooler.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific cafes are not listed for every route, many routes pass through picturesque villages and small towns. Oliva de la Frontera itself, as well as other settlements like Jerez de los Caballeros or Villanueva del Fresno, offer opportunities to find cafes, restaurants, and local shops for refreshments and supplies.

Q: What are the typical distances and durations for road cycling routes here?
A: The routes vary significantly in length and duration. You can find rides ranging from approximately 61 km, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31466014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zahínos Tower loop&lt;/a&gt; which takes around 3 hours, to much longer and more challenging routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Higuera la Real – Convent of the Poor Clares loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 121 km and can take more than 6 hours to complete.

Q: What makes the dehesa landscape unique for road cycling in this area?
A: The 'dehesa' pastureland, which covers over 100,000 hectares in the region, provides a unique cycling experience. It features gentle, rolling shapes, open fields, and scattered oak forests (cork oak and holm oak groves). This landscape offers a tranquil and scenic backdrop for road cycling, often with smooth asphalt roads and minimal traffic, making it perfect for long-distance rides.

Q: Is Oliva de la Frontera a good destination for birdwatching while cycling?
A: Yes, the area is recognized as an Important Bird Area in Europe. While cycling through the dehesa and sierra environments, you might spot various species, including griffon vultures, black vultures, and numerous passerines, adding an extra dimension to your outdoor experience.
tips: The symbol of the town is the only standing tower of the old Zahínos fortress dating from the 15th century and located at the highest point of the town.

Valle de Matamoros is a small and charming municipality in the province of Badajoz, in the autonomous community of Extremadura, located in the Sierra Suroeste region, very close to Jerez de los Caballeros.

It lies on the eastern slope of the Sierra de San José mountain range, with altitudes ranging from 550 to 670 meters.
Its rugged terrain gives it a unique urban layout, with steep streets and natural viewpoints along the village's access roads.

Jerez de los Caballeros is a town with a rich historical legacy, located in the southwest of the province of Badajoz, Extremadura.
Ancient Origins: Its history dates back to the Phoenicians, passing through Roman, Visigothic, Muslim, and Christian periods. It was known as Ceret, Fama Iulia, Xerixa, and finally Jerez de los Caballeros after the Reconquista.
Templars: In the 13th century, the Knights Templar took control of the town, leaving an indelible mark on its architecture and culture.
Historic Center: Declared a Historic-Artistic Site, it is notable for its Baroque churches, medieval walls, cobbled streets, and stately homes.
